articles.title: Phages could enable us to hijack vaccine immunity to kill cancer cells
articles.summary: Phages, viruses that infect bacteria, could be genetically manipulated to destroy cancerous cells using the immunity we have acquired from vaccines

articles.title: NASA is paying $30 million for a 1st-of-its-kind rescue mission to the aging Swift telescope before it falls from space. Is it worth it?
articles.summary: NASA's daring Swift Boost mission launches June 27 to save the nearly 22-year-old Swift observatory , which is being dragged out of space by Earth's atmosphere.

articles.title: James Webb Space Telescope catches 6 galaxies merging into one of the largest galaxies in the universe
articles.summary: A supermassive black hole is also coming together at the heart of this galaxy maelstrom.

articles.title: Partners, NASA Ready for June Launch of Swift Boost Mission
articles.summary: A mission to raise the orbit of NASA’s Swift observatory is poised for launch June 30.

articles.title: Primate evolution kept aging rates stable for 25 million years despite lifespan gaps
articles.summary: Biologists group animals with similar traits into broad categories called orders. Despite their similarities, animal species in the same order can have very different average lifespans.

articles.title: “Absolutely huge” 400-year-old black coral stuns scientists in New Zealand

articles.title: Lamprey brain atlas reveals 450-million-year blueprint of vertebrate brains
articles.summary: What did the very first complex vertebrate brain look like? To find out, scientists turned to an unlikely time traveler: the lamprey, a jawless, eel-like fish whose body plan has barely changed in roughly 360 million…

articles.title: UK sets new June temperature record for third day in a row: Met Office
articles.summary: The UK broke the record for a June temperature for the third day in a row on Friday, the Met Office weather agency said, as a sweltering heat wave strained schools and hospitals and drove down business.

articles.title: Germany sees hottest temperature on record of 41.3C: Weather service
articles.summary: Germany saw its highest temperature ever recorded Friday at 41.3°C (106.3°F), according to preliminary weather service data, as the country braces for the possibility of even higher temperatures over the weekend.

articles.title: Thawing ground, future questions: Decoding Arctic climate in a lab
articles.summary: In a Penn State lab, a small cylinder of soil sits wired with sensors, slowly cooling as it mimics conditions thousands of miles away.

articles.title: Lost books by ancient philosophers recovered from 'unreadable' scrolls
articles.summary: Scrolls from the Roman library of Herculaneum that were carbonised by a volcanic eruption have been read in their entirety for the first time, thanks to scans and AI software

articles.title: Investigative interviews are key to solving crimes—should AI be helping police with their inquiries?
articles.summary: Investigative interviewing—the process of obtaining accurate and complete accounts from victims, witnesses and suspects—is the lifeblood of the criminal justice system.

articles.title: Only a few hours left! This expert-approved, beginner-friendly drone is $60 off for Prime Day
articles.summary: Drone expert James Abbott thinks this DJI Neo is perfect for beginners. $139 for this drone idea for newcomers is a steal.

articles.title: Swiss glaciers facing drastic loss from heat wave: Expert
articles.summary: Swiss glaciers are set to lose an enormous amount of ice due to the heat wave battering Europe, the head of Glacier Monitoring in Switzerland (GLAMOS) told AFP.

articles.title: Farmers fear drought as Italy's longest river runs dry
articles.summary: Seawater is seeping into Italy's longest river as the waterway starts to run dry in the heat wave, hitting a farming heartland that produces the milk for Parmesan cheese.

articles.title: Plasma and graphene combine to protect metal surfaces from corrosion

articles.title: Europe’s heatwave is the hottest and most humid ever
articles.summary: The current temperatures in western and central Europe would have been virtually impossible 50 years ago, and unprecedented humidity levels make this heatwave especially dangerous

articles.title: If you aren't terrified by this heatwave, you should be
articles.summary: The extreme heat currently being felt in Europe isn’t the new normal – much worse is to come, and we are doing far too little to adapt, says Michael Le Page
